coredata.dc:title: Er-doped carbon dots broadening light absorption range and accelerating electron transport for enhancing photovoltaic performance of CdS quantum dots sensitized cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Cheng
Zhang, Xu
Shu, Xiaoqing
Liu, Xudong
Fang, Dawei
Song, Youtao
Wang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, Er-doped carbon quantum dots (Er-CQDs) is first incorporated into CdS quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) for broadening light absorption range and accelerating electron transport. Er-CQDs are prepared by hydrothermal method forming Er-CQDs/TiO2 film. The CdS quantum dots (QDs) are deposited on Er-CQDs/TiO2 film by successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) method, constituting CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode. The structural and optical properties of the CQDs and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode are characterized by FT-IR, UV–vis, FL and PL techniques. The composition and morphology are confirmed by XRD, EDX, SEM and TEM techniques. The optical properties of the influence of Er-CQDs on photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs is studied by electrochemical methods. The experiment data show that the photovoltaic performance of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 working electrode can greatly be enhanced compared that of CdS/TiO2. In comparison, the PCE increases from 0.34% of CdS/TiO2 device to 0.43% of CdS/CQDs/TiO2 device, whose escalating rate is 26.45%. Furthermore, the PCE increases from 0.34% of CdS/TiO2 device to 0.59% of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 device, whose escalating rate reaches 73.53%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Er-doped carbon dots
Light absorption range
Electron transport
CdS quantum dots
Quantum dot-sensitized solar cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdS/TiO2, CdS/CQDs/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes
Preparation of electrolyte, counter electrode and solar cell devices
Optical properties of as-prepared CQDs and Er-CQDs
XRD, SEM, EDX and TEM of prepared samples
Optical properties of TiO2, CdS QDs, CdS/TiO2, CdS/CQDs/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes
Photoluminescence spectra of CdS QDs and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2
Photovoltaic performance and charge transfer behavior of CdS QDSSCs
Working principle of CdS QDSSCs based on CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory Basic Research Foundation of Liaoning Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors greatly acknowledge the National Science Foundation of China ( 21371084 ) and the Key Laboratory Basic Research Foundation of Liaoning Provincial Education Department ( L2015043 ) for financial support. The authors also thank our colleagues and other students for their participation in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectra of as-prepared Er-CQDs and CQDs.
Fluorescent spectra of as-prepared CQDs (a) and Er-CQDs (b-1 and b-2).
XRD patterns of TiO2 (a), CdS QDs (b), CdS/TiO2 (c) and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 (d).
SEM images of cross-sectional view (a) and top view (b and c) of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film.
EDX spectrum of as-prepared CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film.

TEM images of Er-CQDs (a-1: 20 nm, a-2: 10 nm and a-3: 5 nm), CdS QDs (b-1: 20 nm, b-2: 10 nm and b-3: 5 nm) and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode (c: 5 nm) and particle size distributions of Er-CQDs (a-4) and CdS QDs (b-4).

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (a and c) of TiO2 and CdS QDs and different photoanodes (CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2, CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2) and band-gap estimation (b) of TiO2 and CdS QDs based on the Kubelka-Munk function.
Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CdS QDs and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 under excitation of 360 nm light.
J-V curves (a), IPCE curves (b) and Nyquist plots (c) of CdS/TiO2, CdS/CQDs/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 based cells.
Schematic diagram of CdS QDSSCs based on CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode.
Photovoltaic parameters of CdS QDSSCs with different photoanodes.

head.ce:title: Er-doped carbon dots broadening light absorption range and accelerating electron transport for enhancing photovoltaic performance of CdS quantum dots sensitized cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, Er-doped carbon quantum dots (Er-CQDs) is first incorporated into CdS quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) for broadening light absorption range and accelerating electron transport. Er-CQDs are prepared by hydrothermal method forming Er-CQDs/TiO2 film. The CdS quantum dots (QDs) are deposited on Er-CQDs/TiO2 film by successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) method, constituting CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode. The structural and optical properties of the CQDs and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode are characterized by FT-IR, UV–vis, FL and PL techniques. The composition and morphology are confirmed by XRD, EDX, SEM and TEM techniques. The optical properties of the influence of Er-CQDs on photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs is studied by electrochemical methods. The experiment data show that the photovoltaic performance of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 working electrode can greatly be enhanced compared that of CdS/TiO2. In comparison, the PCE increases from 0.34% of CdS/TiO2 device to 0.43% of CdS/CQDs/TiO2 device, whose escalating rate is 26.45%. Furthermore, the PCE increases from 0.34% of CdS/TiO2 device to 0.59% of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 device, whose escalating rate reaches 73.53%.

Er-doped carbon quantum dots (Er-CQDs) is first incorporated into CdS quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) for broadening light absorption range and accelerating electron transport. The CdS quantum dots (QDs) are deposited on Er-CQDs/TiO2 film by successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) method, constituting CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode. The influence of Er-CQDs on photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs is studied by electrochemical methods. The experiment data show that the photovoltaic performance of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 working electrode can greatly be enhanced. The PCE increases from 0.34% of CdS/TiO2 device to 0.43% of CdS/CQDs/TiO2 device. Furthermore, the PCE increases from 0.34% of CdS/TiO2 device to 0.59% of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 device.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) have been considered as next generation of solar cell and attracted worldwide scientific and technological interests because of their unique advantages [1,2], for instance, distinctive electronic and optical properties [3–5], low production cost [6,7] and simple manufacturing process [8,9]. In addition, the theoretical power conversion efficiency (PCE) of QDSSCs can reach 44%, which surpasses the Shockley-Queisser limit based on the hot electron extraction and multi exciton generation effect [10,11]. In the past decades, the worldwide demand for energy is increasing constantly and the use of conventional energy, i.e. fossil fuel, has caused serious environmental problems, such as acid rain, haze and greenhouse effect, which promote the rapid development of QDSSCs [12–16]. Furthermore, developing high performance of QDSSCs is one of the most promising methods to meet the imperative societal need for sustainable clean energy with minimal or no detrimental environment hazards [17–19]. So far, many quantum dots (QDs), such as CdS [20–22], PbS [23], CdTe [24] and CdSe [25], have been utilized in QDSSCs. Among them, CdS QDs have the advantages of appropriate band gap (2.4 eV) to absorb ultraviolet light and partial visible-light in solar light [26], tunable optical property [27] and simple preparation process [28], which makes CdS QDs become an attractive sensitizer in QDSSCs. Besides, the conduction band edge of CdS is higher than that of TiO2, which is beneficial to the injection of photo-generated electrons from the conduction band of CdS QDs to that of TiO2 [29]. With the development of the technology of the QDSSCs, the photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs based on TiO2 photoanode has been greatly improved. However, so far it is still much lower than that people expected. At present stage, many problems still restrict the enhancement of photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs. Firstly, the light absorption capacity of CdS is small and the light absorption range is narrow. Secondly, the charge separation is incomplete and electron collection efficiency is low. Several approaches have been applied to broaden the light absorption range, for example, the introduction of metal nanoparticles (e.g. Au, Pt and Ag NPs) [30–32] and scattering layer [33,34]. Furthermore, in some studies 3D graphene networks or graphene nanosheets have been introduced into CdS QDSSCs to solve the problems of charge separation and collection [35]. It is regret that, in above methods, these problems can not be considered at the same time. The introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the QDSSCs may solve the above problems simultaneously.

As a novel carbon nanomaterial, typically, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the diameter of 2–10 nm. Due to low toxicity, strong and tunable fluorescence luminescence properties and good electron transfer ability, CQDs have attracted much attention in many research fields, such as photocatalysis, bioimaging and chemical sensing [36–38]. Up to now, many methods have been reported to prepare CQDs, which can be roughly classified into “top-down” synthetic route, including laser ablation [39] and electrochemical etching [40], and “bottom-up” approaches, such as, hydrothermal/solvethermal method [41] and microwave synthetic route [42]. The synthesized CQDs possess high optical properties, which often exhibits good absorption from ultraviolet-light to near visible light, emitting strong blue light. Some approaches have been used to further improve the optical property, for example, doping of nonmetal elements [43]. Based on the unique luminescent property, some rare earth elements can be applied to enhance the luminescent properties of CQDs. In special crystal field, some rare earth metal ions (for example: Er3+) display the up and down-conversion luminescent functions simultaneously. Thus, it can be expected that the CQDs, through doping Er3+ forming Er-doped CQDs (Er-CQDs), can achieve up and down-conversion luminescent functions. Triethylene tetramine hexaacetic acid (ttha), a decadentate aminopolycarboxylic acid ligand, has six carboxyl groups and four amino groups [44]. Thus, Er3+ ion can form a stable nine-coordinate complex with five O atoms and four N atoms from one ttha ligand. Apparently, the ttha ligand can provide a special crystal field around Er3+ ion for up and down-conversion luminescence. When the Er3+-ttha coordinate complex is used to prepare the Er-CQDs, the corresponding crystal field can be preserved, which makes Er-CQDs still possess up and down-convention luminescent functions. Thus, in this study the Er3+-ttha coordinate complex is selected to prepare the Er-CQDs. According to the above description, on the one hand, the synthesized Er-CQDs can emit blue-light under irradiation of ultraviolet light. On the other hand, it can also up-convert the long wavelength light into short wavelength light in blue light range. The obtained blue lights can effectively excite the electrons from the valance band of CdS QDs to the conduction band. Moreover, the Er-CQDs with conjugated system can improve the charge transport from the CdS QD to the TiO2. Therefore, it would be expected that the photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs based on TiO2 film could be enhanced after the incorporation of Er-CQDs.

In this study, CdS/TiO2/FTO, CdS/CQDs/TiO2/FTO and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2/FTO photoanodes were prepared by sol-gel, spin-coating and successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) method, respectively. The morphology, composition and optical property of the samples and the photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs were investigated in detail. Besides, the working principle of CdS QDSSCs based on CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode was proposed. The effects of introducing Er-CQDs and CQDs on the photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs were also studied by photocurrent-voltage characteristics. The experiment results showed that, after adding the Er-CQDs, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of CdS QDSSCs could be enhanced obviously.

Triethylene tetramine hexaacetic acid (C18H30N4O12, 99.999%), erbium oxide power (Er2O3, 99.999%) and ammonium Hydroxide (NH3·H2O, 25–28%) were used to synthetize the CQDs and Er-CQDs. Tetrabutyl titanate (C16H36O4Ti, 98.0%), nitric acid (HNO3, 65–68%) and ethanol absolute (C2H6O, 99.7%) were purchase from Aladdin to prepare TiO2 film. Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O, 98.0%), cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2, 99.0%) and methanol (CH3OH, 99.5%) were applied to deposit CdS QDs on TiO2 film. Cupric sulfate anhydrous (CuSO4, 99.0%) and sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O, 98.0%) were used to prepare counter electrode. Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O, 98.0%), sulfur power (S, 99.9%) and potassium chloride (KCl, 99.8%) was used to prepare polysulfide electrolyte solution. F-doped tin oxide glass (FTO, 8 Ω sq−1) was used as substrate in the process of fabricating device. Deionized water was also used for all. F-doped tin oxide conductive glass (FTO, 8 Ω sq−1) was used as substrates. All chemicals were used without further puriﬁcation.

The simple methods, backflow method and hydrothermal method, were used to synthesize Er-CQDs. In a typical procedure, a 2.47 g C18H30N4O12 was added to 100 mL deionized water. Then an appropriate amount of Er2O3 power was mixed in bathes. The mixture was stirred and refluxed for 72 h, the pH was adjusted to 5.0 by NH3 aqueous after the solution became transparent and then concentrated the solution to 50 mL. Finally, the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h in a vacuum oven. After the reaction, the Teflon-sealed autoclave was cooled to room temperature to obtain the Er-CQDs aqueous solution. The Er-CQDs aqueous solution was purified by filtering through a dialysis bags. Compared with the preparation of the Er-CQDs, the CQDs was prepared by hydrothermal method. First at all, 2.47 g C18H30N4O12 was added to 100 mL deionized water then transferred the mixture to the 100 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave. The mixture was heated at 180 °C for 10 h in a vacuum oven to synthetize the CQDs aqueous solution. The CQDS aqueous solution was also purified by filtering through a dialysis bags.

The TiO2 film was grown on FTO substrate by sol-gel spin-coating method. First, 8.5 mL tetrabutyl titanate was added into 15.0 mL ethanol absolute and stirred vigorously for 30 min marked as solution A. 0.45 mL deionized water and a few drops HNO3 were added into another 20.0 mL ethanol absolute and then intensely stirred for 30 min, which is marked as solution B. Then, solution A was added dropwise into the solution B. After stirred for 5 h, the mixture was concentrated to 30.0 mL and formed a transparent sol. After that, the TiO2 film was grown on FTO substrate by spin-coating at 1800 rpm for 20 s. The film was dried at 80 °C for 30 min. The spin-coating process was totally repeated four times. Finally, the as prepared films were placed in a muffle furnace and maintained 550 °C for 1.0 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdS/TiO2, CdS/CQDs/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: The CdS QDs were sensitized on the TiO2 film, which were immersed in deionized water, aqueous CQDs solution and aqueous Er-CQDs solution by SILAR method [7]. In this method, for the preparation of CdS/TiO2 photoanode, the TiO2 film was dipped into 0.05 M Cd(NO3)2 aqueous methanol solution (1:1, v/v) for 1 min to make the TiO2 film absorb Cd2+ ions and rinsed with methanol to remove the excess Cd2+ ions. Then, the film was dried by an infrared lamp. After that, the film was dipped for another 1 min into 0.05 M Na2S aqueous methanol solution (1:1, v/v) to allow the pre-absorbed Cd2+ ions to react with S2− ions, which caused the information of Cd QDs. Next, the film was rinsed again with methanol dried by infrared lamp. Above two dipping procedures were considered one cycle. The amount of CdS QDs deposited on TiO2 film can be altered by changing the assembly cycle and eight cycles were applied in our research. The CdS/CQDs/TiO2, CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes were prepared by the same process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electrolyte, counter electrode and solar cell devices
ce:section.ce:para: Dissolving S, Na2S·9H2O and KCl with mole ratios of 1:1:0.1 in methanol-water (3:7, v/v) solution made the polysulfide electrolyte [14]. The counter electrode is a nanostructured Cu2S film on FTO substrate, made by chemical bath deposition (CBD) method. Before adding the agents, argon was flowed through the deionized water for 30 min at least to purge air from the deionized water. The 0.37 g Na2S2O3·5H2O and 0.24 g Cu(SO4)2·5H2O were dissolved in the deionized water. Then, the FTO glass, which was cleaned successively by deionized water, alcohol and acetone, was immersed into the solution and kept at 80 °C for 1.0 h. The CdS/TiO2, CdS/CQDs/TiO2 or CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode and a Cu2S-coated FTO counter electrode were assembled into a sandwich-type cell and penetrated with a polysulfide electrolyte.

The prepared samples were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, D-8, Bruker-axs, Germany) using Ni filtered Cu Kα radiation in the range of 2θ from 10° to 80°, scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-5610LV, Hitachi Corporation, Japan), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX, JEOL JSM-5610LV, Hitachi Corporation, Japan) spectroscopy integrated in SEM, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM2100, Hitachi Corporation, Japan), UV–vis absorption spectra (UV–vis, UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan), photoluminescence (PL, RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan) spectra and fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bio, FIS-165, America).

The determination of FT-IR spectrum was carried out to study the surface functional groups of as-prepared Er-CQDs. Fig. 1 displays broadly characteristic absorption peaks at 3411.76 cm−1 and 3159.87 cm−1, which come from the stretching vibrations of C-OH of hydroxyl groups and C-N vibrations, respectively. The sharp peaks at 1596.73 cm−1 and 1401.05 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C=O in carboxyl groups. The sharp peak at 1095.33 cm−1 is corresponding to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of C-N-C. The above results are similar with the reported values [45]. Because the presence of C=O and C-N-C groups, Er3+ ion can form a stable complex with five O atoms and four N atoms, which can provide a special crystal field around Er3+ ion for up and down-conversion luminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of as-prepared CQDs and Er-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3(a), the strongest emission peaks locate at 400–550 nm and that shifts toward longer wavelength when the CQDs are excited by the excitation light from 350 nm to 500 nm. More specifically, the wavelength corresponding to the emission peaks under excitation wavelength from 400 nm to 500 nm has deviated from the absorption of the CdS QDs. From Figs. 3(b-1), it can be seen that the emission peaks of Er-CQDs locate at about 450 nm under excitation of light from 350 nm to 450 nm. Compared with the CQDs, the Er-CQDs displays much higher emission intensities under the excitation of same wavelength light. As shown in Figs. 3(b-2), the excitation wavelength is broadened to 550 nm due to the introduction Er to CQDs, which is consistent with the absorption spectrum of Er-CQDs in Fig. 1. Besides, it can also be observed that some emission peaks concentrate around 450 nm when the excitation wavelengths are altered from 450 nm to 550 nm. That is, Er-CQDs have the ability to convert long wavelengths (550 nm) of light into short wavelengths (450 nm) of light, which is called as up-conversion luminescence. From the above results, it can be confirmed that Er-CQDs have the dual function (down and up) convention luminescence. That is, the emission peaks of Er-CQDs concentrate at 450 nm under the excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 550 nm, which should effectively excite CdS QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD, SEM, EDX and TEM of prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The crystal structures of TiO2, CdS QDs, CdS/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 were characterized by X-ray diffractions (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4(a) shows diffraction peaks at 2θ = 25.15°, 33.21°, 48.46°, 54.32°, 55.52°, 62.51°, 69.36°, 70.55° and 75.37°, which correspond to (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220) and (215) lattice planes of anatase crystal structure of TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21–1272), respectively. However, a low peak at 2θ = 27.52° is also found, which is main diffraction peak of (110) lattice plane of rutile crystal structure of TiO2. From the XRD pattern of prepared TiO2, it can be seen that the anatase phase is the predominant structure. The weight fractions of each phase are estimated by using following Eqs (1) and (2), respectively.
(1)
W A
=
A A
0.884
A R
+
A A
(2)
W R
=
A R
0.884
A A
+
A R
Where WA and WR is the weight fraction of anatase and rutile TiO2, respectively, AA represents the integrated peak intensity of the anatase (101) plane and AR represents the integrated peak intensity of the rutile (110) plane. The calculated weight fractions of anatase and rutile TiO2 are 94.86% and 5.14%, respectively. The average particle size of anatase TiO2 can be calculated to be 26 nm by utilizing the Scherrer's formula (3).
(3)
L =
K λ
β
cos
θ
Where L is the crystallite size, K is a constant (usually 0.89), λ is the wavelength of the X-ray radiation (0.15418 nm for Cu Kα), β is the full-width at half-maximum and θ is the corresponding diffraction angle in degrees. The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 26.53°, 44.62° and 52.52° can be observed in Fig. 4(b), reflecting from (110), (220) and (311) crystal planes of cubic lattice of CdS QDs, respectively, which match well with the standard card data of cubic CdS (JCPDS No. 10–0454). The relatively broad width of the diffraction peaks reveals the small size of the CdS QDs, which can be proved by calculated particle size (4.1 nm) of CdS QDs by applying Scherrer's formula. In Fig. 4(c) three extra peaks at 2θ = 26.17°, 44.62° and 52.54° corresponding to (110), (220) and (311) lattice plane of cubic lattice of CdS QDs can be observed. In comparison to TiO2 in Fig. 4(a), it can be confirmed that the CdS QDs have been deposited on TiO2. Fig. 4(d) shows that all diffraction patterns of TiO2 and CdS QDs are similar with the standard card data of TiO2 and CdS, respectively, which implies that introduction of Er-CQDs hardly affects the phase structures of TiO2 and CdS QDs. The main characteristic peak of Er-CQDs at 2θ = 25.52° overlaps with that of anatase TiO2, resulting in an increase in peak intensity at 2θ = 25.52°, which proves that Er-CQDs have been adsorbed upon the surface of TiO2. The peaks of TiO2 and CdS QDs can obviously be seen in Fig. 4(d), which reveals that CdS QDs have been combined well with TiO2.

The cross-sectional and surface morphologies of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film were evaluated by SEM. Fig. 5 (a) shows the SEM image of cross-sectional view of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film. It is clear that the thickness of the prepared CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film is about 4.78 μm. Moreover, it also can be seen that the CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film is compact, which is favorable for the electron transfer from the conduction band of CdS QDs to that of TiO2. The top view of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film is presented in Fig. 5(b) (1000 nm scale) and c (500 nm scale)). From the Fig. 5(b), it can be seen that the surface of the CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film through 8 cycles SILAR deposition of CdS QDs on Er-CQDs/TiO2/FTO surface is rough. In Fig. 5(c) it can be estimated that the diameter of TiO2 nanoparticles is about 25 nm, which is similar to the calculated result based on XRD studies. In addition, it can also be found that TiO2 nanoparticles have been well distributed on FTO substrate. The sizes of CdS QDs and Er-CQDs are both too small to be seen clearly in Fig. 5(b and c). Furthermore, there are many inner pores in CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film and that these inner pores are not large, which may be because the CdS QDs and Er-CQDs are deposited on the surface of inner pores of TiO2. The inner pores enable electrolyte to well penetrate the photoanode, which results in a satisfactory interfacial contact between electrolyte and CdS QDs in the CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 cell.

In order to determine the element composition of the prepared CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoannode, the determination of Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy was carried out. The EDX spectrum of as-prepared CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film is shown in Fig. 6 . It can be found that the prepared CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 film is composed of Cd, S, C, N, Ti and O elements. The atomic percentages of Cd and S are estimated to be 1.10% and 1.96%, respectively, which is close to the stoichiometric composition of CdS. The result further indicates that the CdS QDs have formed and been deposited on the surface of Er-CQDs/TiO2 film. According to the preparation process of Er-CQDs/TiO2 film the Er-CQDs containing oxygen element should be absorbed on the surface of TiO2 film, which causes the atomic ratio of O and Ti to be larger than 2. Moreover, the appearance of the peaks belonging to Er element in the spectrum also reveals that the Er-CQDs have been synthetized successfully.

The crystal structures of Er-CQDs, CdS QDs and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 were investigated by TEM. The TEM images of Er-CQDs are shown in Fig. 7 (a-1, a-2 and a-3). It can be found that there are many small dark black dots with uniform particle size in Figs. 7(a-1). Fig. 7(a-1) also reveals good monodispersity feature of Er-CQDs. After amplifying the Figs. 7(a-1), spherical nanoparticles in shape known as the as-prepared Er-CQDs can clearly be seen in Figs. 7(a-2). Besides, the particle sizes of the Er-CQDs particles are less than 5 nm. In Figs. 7(a-3), the lattice spacing is measured to be 0.247 nm, which is closed to the reported value (0.25 nm) indexed to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon of the CQDs [46]. In other words, introduction of Er into CQDs has not led to an obvious change in the crystal structure. In order to obtain the size distribution histogram of Er-CQDs, one hundred particles are randomly selected and then measure the particle size. The size distribution of Er-CQDs is shown in Figs. 7(a-4). It can be found that the diameters of Er-CQDs particles distribute in the range from 2.5 nm to 5.5 nm. The average size of as-prepared Er-CQDs can be calculated to be 3.60 ± 0.40 nm. The TEM images of prepared CdS QDs are shown in Figs. 7(b-1, b-2 and b-3). Figs. 7(b-1) shows that CdS QDs is also spherical nanoparticles in shape. From Figs. 7(b-2), it can be observed that the size of CdS QDs are bigger than that of Er-CQDs. Furthermore, lattice fringe of 0.175 nm corresponding to the (311) plane of cubic CdS can be obtained. The value is similar with that of previous literature [47], which indicates that the CdS QDs have been successfully prepared. The size distribution histogram of CdS QDs is obtained by the same method as above. It is clearly found in Figs. 7(b-4) that the prepared CdS QDs have the size distribution in 4.07 ± 0.18 nm. The crystal structure of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 can be observed in Fig. 7(c). The 0.164 nm lattice fringe can be observed, which can be ascribed to (211) plane of anatase TiO2. The 0.247 nm fringe is attributed to the (100) plane of Er-CQDs. Moreover, 0.206 nm and 0.332 nm fringes can be seen, which correspond to the (220) and (111) planes of cubic CdS, respectively. Because the Er- CQDs are absorbed by the TiO2 particles, it can be found that the Er-CQDs are on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. It also can be found that some CdS QDs are attached to the Er-CQDs, indicating that the CdS QDs have been deposited on the surface of Er-CQDs/TiO2 film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of TiO2, CdS QDs, CdS/TiO2, CdS/CQDs/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of TiO2, CdS QDs and different photoanodes (CdS/TiO2, CdS/CQDs/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2) were investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. 8 (a–c)). As shown in Fig. 8(a), TiO2 nanoparticles can only absorb the high energy light with shorter wavelength below 400 nm. For CdS QDs, the steep absorption edge approximately locates at 550 nm, which is closely related to the intrinsic narrow band-gap absorption of CdS (2.4 eV). As displayed in Fig. 8(b), the band-gaps of TiO2 and CdS QDs are estimated by extrapolating the linear portion of the (αhν)2 versus Energy plots, according to the following Eq (4):
(4)
(
α h ν
)
2
= c
(
h ν − E g
)

Where α, c, ν and h are the absorbance, a constant, frequency and Plank's constant, respectively [10]. From the Fig. 8(b), the band gaps of TiO2 and CdS QDs corresponding to absorption edge are about 2.98 eV and 2.23 eV, respectively, which are similar with the reported values [18,46]. Fig. 8(c) shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CdS/TiO2, CdS/CQDs/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes ranging from 300 to 800 nm. An absorption shoulder peak appears at visible region in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CdS/TiO2 photoanode, which is attributed to the absorption of CdS QDs. That is, after depositing CdS QDs, the light absorption range of CdS/TiO2 photoanode is broadened to visible light region. Compared with CdS/TiO2 photoanode, the light absorption intensity of CdS/CQDs/TiO2 increases ranging in 300–480 nm but reduces in 480–500 nm. For CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode, not only the light absorption intensity increases, but also the light absorption range red-shifts compared with that of CdS/TiO2 photoanode. This is because the CQDs only absorb ultraviolet light. However, Er-CQDs can absorb not only ultraviolet light, but also the visible-light ranging from 480 nm to 550 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence spectra of CdS QDs and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the charge separation, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CdS QDs and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 are tested and the results are shown in Fig. 9 . When the CdS QDs are excited under 360 nm light, a strong emission peak appears at 525 nm in PL spectrum, indicating that as-prepared CdS QDs have relatively high recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. When the CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 are excited by using the same wavelength light, a weak PL emission peak at 525 nm can be found, which is much lower compared with that of CdS QDs. This weak PL emission peak suggests that the CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 has low recombination rate of electrons and holes after depositing Er-CQDs on TiO2 film. It also indicates that the CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 device has higher charge extraction compared to CdS/TiO2 device, which allows more photo-generated electrons to transfer from the conduction band of CdS QDs to that of TiO2. Thus, the photovoltaic performance of the QDSSCs based on CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode can be enhanced. Besides, in PL spectrum of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 there are also some other low emission peaks at 425–475 nm, 550–575 nm and 600–625 nm, respectively, which come from the down convention luminescences of Er-CQDs. It further indicates that the Er-CQDs display the down convention luminescence characteristics, which can be applied well by CdS QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photovoltaic performance and charge transfer behavior of CdS QDSSCs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to investigate the effect of the introduction of CQDs and Er-CQDs to TiO2 film on the photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs, the photocurrent-voltage characteristics were studied under one sun light condition (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW/cm2) in
S
2 −
/
S n
2 −
electrolyte system. According to Fig. 10 (a), the relative photovoltaic parameters including short circuit current density (Jsc), open circuit voltage (Voc), fill factor (FF) and power conversion efficiency (PCE) are summarized in Table 1 . It can be seen from Table 1 that, for CdS/TiO2 QDSSCs, the open circuit voltage (Voc) and the short circuit current density (Jsc) are 0.52 V and 1.30 mA/cm2, respectively. For CdS/CQDs/TiO2 QDSSCs, the open circuit voltage (Voc) and the short circuit current density (Jsc) reach to 0.53 V and 1.60 mA/cm2, respectively. And that, for CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 QDSSCs, the open circuit voltage (Voc) and short circuit current (Jsc) density are 0.53 V and 2.25 mA/cm2, respectively. Apparently, after adding the CQDs and Er-CQDs in the TiO2 film, the open circuit voltage and the short circuit current density of CdS QDSSCs are both enhanced compared with that of CdS/TiO2 device. Particularly, the CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 device exhibits the highest open circuit voltage (Voc) and biggest short circuit current density (Jsc). Furthermore, the Eq (5) is used to describe the influence of CQDs and Er-CQDs in the CdS QDSSCs.
(5)
V
o c
=
K T
q
ln
J
i n j
n
c b
k r
[
S n
2 −
]
Where K, T, q, Jinj, ncb, kr and
[
S n
2 −
]
are Boltzmann constant, absolute temperature, electronic elementary charge, electron injection current caused in the CdS QDs, electron concentration of the conduction band of TiO2, the electron recombination rate and
S n
2 −
concentration in the electrolyte, respectively. In this study,
[
S n
2 −
]
is considered a constant because of the same electrolyte. From Eq (5), it can be found that the Voc displays a positive correlation to Jinj and negative correlation to ncb and kr. The introduction of CQDs or Er-CQDs makes the values of ncb and kr decrease because of the enhancement of electron transport rate and inhibition of charge recombination. According to the Equation Jinj = qAI0Φinj (q: electronic elementary charge, I0: incident photon flux, A: ratio of absorbed photon flux to I0, Φinj: quantum yield for electrons injection from conduction band of CdS QDs into the conduction band of TiO2), the influencing factors on Jinj can be obtained. In this study, the I0 is assumed to be fixed because of the same irradiation condition. Therefore, the Jinj is positively relative to A and Φinj. Due to the incorporation of Er-CQDs or CQDs into CdS/TiO2 photoanode, the light absorption of the photoanode and the electron transport from CdS QDs to TiO2 can be improved, which causes the increase of A and Φinj. According to above illustrations, it can be confirmed that the introduction of CQDs or Er-CQDs can lead to an obvious increase in open circuit voltage. In addition, the PCE of completed cells is raised after introduction of CQDs or Er-CQDs into CdS QDs sensitized TiO2 photoanode. In comparison, the PCE increases from 0.34% of CdS/TiO2 device to 0.43% of CdS/CQDs/TiO2 device, whose escalating rate is 26.45%. However, the PCE increases from 0.34% of CdS/TiO2 device to 0.59% of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 device, whose escalating rate reaches 73.53%. In can be confirmed that the obvious improvement in PCE of CdS QDSSCs comes from the increase of J sc and V oc caused by Er-CQDs.

The incident photon to current convention efficiency (IPCE) can be used to analyze the light harvest efficiency. The IPCE curves of the CdS QDSSCs with different photoanodes are shown in Fig. 10(b). It can be seen that the CdS QDSSCs based on CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode exhibits the maximum IPCE value around 450 nm. In addition, for these three photoanodes the IPCE values of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode are also the highest in range from 320 nm to 600 nm. The IPCE values for the CdS/TiO2 photoanode are the lowest at the same range. The IPCE values for CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode increase in the range from 320 nm to 400 nm. It is because Er-CQDs can emit blue light by absorbing the light in the range from 320 nm to 400 nm, which can excite the CdS QDs. Besides, the IPCE values of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode also increase in the range from 450 nm to 550 nm, which is because Er-CQDs can absorb low-energy visible-lights ranging from 450 nm to 550 nm and emit high-energy blue-lights and those lights can effectively excite the CdS QDs. The increment of IPCE values of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode in range from 320 nm to 600 nm is also attributed to the excellent electrical conductivity of the Er-CQDs, which can enhance the electron transfer and inhibit the charge recombination. Thus, introduction Er-CQDs into CdS QDSSCs can enhance the photovoltaic performance.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is useful to study charge transfer resistance at photoanode/electrolyte and counter electrode/electrolyte interface. EIS determination was carried out at a forward bias of 0.53 V with a frequency range of 0.01 Hz–100 kHz. Fig. 10(c) displays the Nyquist plots and the equivalent circuit of the CdS QDSSCs based on CdS/TiO2, CdS/CQDs/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes. It can be seen that, for CdS/TiO2, CdS/CQDs/TiO2 and CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes, there are all two semicircles. The semicircle at higher frequency is corresponding to the charge transfer resistance in the Cu2S counter electrode/electrolyte interface. The other semicircle at lower frequency is corresponding to the charge transfer resistance in the photoanode/electrolyte interface. Because of the same Cu2S counter electrode and electrolyte, for these three photoanodes their semicircles at higher frequency are similar. Compared with CdS/TiO2 and CdS/CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes, the CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode exhibits the lowest charge transfer resistance in the photoanode/electrolyte interface, indicating that electrons can transfer faster through the CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode/electrolyte interface after the introduction of Er-CQDs. The reason is that the Er-CQDs with highly conductive property can act as highly effective electron transfer channel, which can enhance electron transport and decrease the charge recombination. Apparently, the reduction of the electron transfer resistance promotes the photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of CdS QDSSCs based on CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic diagram of CdS QDSSCs based on CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode is shown in Fig. 11 . CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode, polysulfide electrolyte and Cu2S counter electrode were assembled into solar cell device using sandwich cell configuration. In this solar cell device, the used Er-CQDs possess the up and down-conversion luminescent functions at the same time. In up-conversion luminescent process, Er-CQDs can absorb low-energy visible-lights ranging from 450 nm to 550 nm and emit high-energy blue-lights with 450 nm wavelength. And that, in down-conversion luminescent process, Er-CQDs can absorb high-energy ultraviolet and near visible-lights and also emit visible-lights (blue-lights). The emitted blue-lights can be absorbed by the CdS QDs in CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 photoanode. In addition, the blue-lights in solar light can directly be absorbed by the CdS QDs. These blue-lights can efficiently excite the electrons of CdS QDs from the valance band to the conduction band, entering the conduction band of TiO2. Therefore, the introduction of Er-CQDs in CdS QDSSCs can greatly enhance the utilization rate of solar light and broaden the light absorption range of CdS QDs. Besides, due to good conductivity, the introduction of the Er-CQDs can cut down the transmission distance of electrons from CdS QDs to TiO2, which enable electrons rapidly transfer from the conduction band of CdS QDs to the conduction band of TiO2. That is, the introduction of Er-CQDs can decrease the recombination rate of photo-generate electron and hole. Furthermore, when the Er-CQDs are deposited on the internal pore surface in TiO2 film, the transmission distance of electrons can also be shortened, which can facilitate the electron transport to reach the FTO conductive glass. The holes on the valence band of CdS QDs can transfer to electrolyte and oxidize the polysulfide. The electrons transport through a wire to the Cu2S counter electrode and then reduce the oxidized polysulfide. Finally, the photovoltaic performances of CdS QDSSCs are studied in the condition of AM = 1.5 G and 100 mW/cm2. The results demonstrate that, due to the presence of Er-CQDs, the short circuit current and open circuit voltage of CdS QDSSCs can be greatly enhanced.

In summary, CQDs and Er-CQDs were successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method. The influences of CQDs and Er-CQDs in CdS QDSSCs on photovoltaic performance were compared and investigated. Through up- and down-conversion luminescences process, Er-CQDs can convert 450–550 nm and 350–450 nm lights in solar lights to 450–480 nm (blue) lights, respectively. Those converted lights can be effectively absorbed by the CdS QDs. In addition, the introduction of Er-CQDs can shorten the transport distance of electrons from CdS QDs to TiO2. Incorporation of Er-CQDs can broaden light absorption range of CdS QDSSCs and facilitate electron transport from CdS QDs to TiO2, which can greatly increase the photovoltaic performance of CdS QDSSCs. The PCE increases from 0.34% of CdS/TiO2 device to 0.43% of CdS/CQDs/TiO2 device, whose escalating rate is 26.45%. However, the PCE increases from 0.34% of CdS/TiO2 device to 0.59% of CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 device, whose escalating rate reaches 73.53%. These results confirm that the introduction of Er-CQDs in CdS QDSSCs can effectively enhance the photovoltaic performance. Perhaps, the CdS/Er-CQDs/TiO2 would become an excellent photoanode in CdS QDSSCs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the National Science Foundation of China (21371084) and the Key Laboratory Basic Research Foundation of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (L2015043) for financial support. The authors also thank our colleagues and other students for their participation in this work.
